th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: History and culture connect the past, present and future.
lo: How technology has changed over time; The purpose and significance of artefacts; How the past connects to the present and future
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Not explicitly stated as "Central Idea," but the unit is structured around understanding how the past, present, and future are connected, and how artefacts, traditions, and technology reflect change over time.

LINES OF INQUIRY: How we are linked to our family members; How the writing characters connect to the family members; How are past, present and futures different from each other; How we change over time; How dance and the role of dance in society has changed, or not changed, over time; Artefacts are gathered over time; We all have different traditions which connect us to our heritage.

KEY CONCEPTS: Connection, Perspective, Change, Time, Heritage

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Discuss the central idea and the definition of past, present and future. Maths activity: Timeline at 5-year increments. Students view and discuss artefacts such as old phones, projectors, encyclopedias, and cassettes. Watch videos showing the evolution of technology (e.g., Phone, Mobile Phone, Television, Walkman Commercial, Discman Commercial, Steve Jobs iPhone Announcement).

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Students bring in photos and artefacts from home to stimulate conversation.
- Sorting photos on a timeline and creating a display.
- Watching and discussing videos about the evolution of technology.
- Creating a timeline of dance with the class.
- Watching BTN 'Old School' clip and discussing findings.
- Final Seesaw activity: students predict what a mobile device will do and look like in the future.
- Chinese: Students create their own family trees in Chinese, labeling relationships.
- Dance and Drama: Comparing and contrasting dances from different eras, dancing in unison, and remembering sequences.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Analyse student contributions to timeline and Venn diagrams.
- Assess written and drawing responses for vocabulary and understanding.
- Assess independent Seesaw tasks (including future predictions).
- Ongoing monitoring of class discussions and ability to identify artefacts, stories, and people.
- Opportunities for self and peer assessment using Venn diagrams to compare dances from different decades.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Students worked together to create timelines of technology and dance, supporting collaboration and ownership.
- Student agency is supported through voice (bringing artefacts/photos), choice (designing future devices), and ownership (timeline creation).
- Artefacts collected from home, library books, and shoe boxes used as resources.
- Technology integration includes Seesaw for assessment and documentation, use of videos (YouTube, BTN), and digital timelines.
- Transdisciplinary connections with Chinese (family trees) and Dance/Drama (historical comparison).
- Action opportunities: Students share conversations about family history and technology at home, and design future technology.
- Integration notes: Opportunities for digital storytelling, multimedia timelines, and collaborative digital projects using Seesaw or similar platforms.

ci: We learn about ourselves through the games we play.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Read Kimochis Story to build knowledge of different emotions.
- URStrong "In your shoes" develop empathy.
- GYM ? Introduction of animals.
- Cybersafety ? Lesson 11 ? How does it make me feel? (Games online).
- Class discussion about the different sports and games they regularly participate in.
- Identify the different ways to build a team identity (e.g., mascots, colour, body language/signals, scoring systems, song, uniform) drawing on examples from major sporting events: Olympics, AFL, NBA and NRL.
- URStrong Continued ? Implementing strategies and a common vocabulary.
- Cyber Safety Continued ? Learning about positive online interactions when playing games.
- Class discussions ? reflecting on real life scenarios in the playground.
- Use strategies every day in the playground.
- Cyber Safety ? Lesson 14 children refer to emotions and URStrong.
- PSPE Create your own team.
- Final Portfolio Piece.

- Monitoring, documenting and measuring learning through URStrong program and playground discussions.
- Observing and responding to game play sessions.
- Monitoring creation of a team that reflects personal identity and describing choices (symbols, colour, mascots, song, uniform, body language/signals).

- Ongoing teacher feedback after play sessions, recess/lunch and other school experiences.
- Documenting learning through a Seesaw workbook supports monitoring.
- Resources include URStrong, Cyber Safety Project, Grow Your Minds, IB Essential Agreements, and Kimochi books from the library.
- Opportunities for self and peer assessment: reflecting within and after game sessions, evaluating communication and behaviour, and adjusting as needed.
- Students played with different people and different games in the classroom and on the playground; some brought in examples of how a favourite team expressed themselves.
- Technology integration: Seesaw for documentation, reflection, and portfolio tasks; possible use of digital tools for creating team identities and reflecting on online game experiences.

ci: Artefacts provide a window into the beliefs and values of cultures.
lo: Beliefs and values contribute to a culture; The importance of rituals and traditions; How artefacts capture history and cultural expression

LINES OF INQUIRY: 
- What is my culture? 
- How do I celebrate? 
- Why do people celebrate all over the world? 
- What are the rituals and traditions that are important to my family? 
- Why do they eat those foods? 
- When do they eat these foods? 
- How can games be an expression of culture? 
- Why do people dance/sing/perform? 
- When do people dance/sing/perform? 
- How is it the same or different?

KEY CONCEPTS: Perspective, Connection, Beliefs, Communication, Expression

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: 
- Visit from Kaylea Fern with artefacts.
- Drawing a picture of a celebration/tradition in your family.
- Chinese New Year celebration: Students explore different traditions and cultural activities people do during CNY.
- Teacher introduction to examples of games linked to different cultures.
- Teaching songs and dances from around the world.
- "Why do People Dance?" YouTube group quiz where students watch videos of different dance styles and cultural dances.
- See, Think, Wonder template.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Exploring what culture is and its components (languages, origins, food, etc.).
- Homework sheet for parents/family to learn about cultural connections and links to sports (questions created by students).
- Investigating the role of music in celebration and passing down traditions.
- Expert groups: students become experts in a particular cultural dance and share with the class.
- Learning and performing a range of cultural dances and dance styles.
- "Save the last word": interpreting and communicating artefacts using a thinking tool.
- Comparing food names in English and Chinese.
- Identifying new/interesting games from homework to play in PE.
- Listening to Bolivian music and comparing instruments.
- Creating their own celebration dances.
- Mini research project on an artefact of personal interest, shared using SPO and WWWWH template, published in a paragraph.
- Affirmation Beads: create and make statements about personal values and beliefs.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Ongoing: Peer assessment (class discussion, Save the last word, Cultural Connections), observation, formative assessment during lessons, documenting (WWWWH personal artefact, mini research, puzzle piece, modeling examples).
- Summative: 2x Seesaw tasks (including mini research and artefact work), plus formative assessment throughout all tasks.
- Opportunities for self and peer assessment: Save the last word, affirmations, puzzle piece.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong transdisciplinary connections: Chinese, PE, Health, Visual Arts, Music, Dance, Drama, English, History, Geography, Social Studies, Digital Technologies.
- Student agency supported through voice (personal artefact, tradition picture, puzzle piece), choice (affirmations, mini research, topic, see/think/wonder, writing piece, student-created homework questions), and ownership (puzzle piece analysis and creation).
- Technology integration: Seesaw for assessment and sharing, PowerPoint for presentations, YouTube for dance quizzes, digital templates for research and reflection.
- Students used affirmation beads for self-regulation and investigated their own and other cultures further in home learning and their own time.
- Resources include Shenanigans Bush Dances of New Holland, Bunjil Our Creator, and various digital/online resources.

- Observation: vocabulary use in individual and group tasks.
- Formative assessment: in-the-moment feedback throughout lessons.
- Summative assessment: persuasive writing.
- Seesaw task: 1st live broadcast & before/after the internet.
- Persuasive writing rubric.
- Portfolios and opportunities for self and peer assessment.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration of digital technologies (robotics, coding, internet history).
- Transdisciplinary connections to previous units (e.g., STP: Human actions change the environment; Living things have structures that allow them to survive).
- Opportunities for student agency: voice (persuasive writing, discussion, coding), choice (comic strip, sequencing), ownership (persuasive writing).
- Action opportunities: students reflect on ethical dilemmas and the impact of technology on society and ecosystems.
- Integration with English (persuasive writing, vocabulary), HASS, and Digital Technologies.
- Use of Seesaw for digital portfolios and assessment.
- Class novel supports perspective-taking and ethical considerations.
- Reflection and metacognition embedded (self-assessment, goal setting, pros and cons discussions).
- Links to sustainability concepts from other units (STP, HTWW).
- Note: For next year, class novel may be changed for sensitivity reasons.

ci: People and the planet are reliant on different energy sources.
lo: Energy types (electricity, fossil fuels, solar); Energy helps us in daily life; Energy transformation; Responsibility to conserve energy and find sustainable sources
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Energy comes in a variety of forms, is everywhere and is used in our everyday lives.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The energy types: electricity, fossil fuels, solar energy; Energy helps us in our daily lives; Energy is transformed from one state to another. Change to clean energy takes time; It is our responsibility to conserve energy and source more sustainable types of energy; Different energy sources are more sustainable than others and this impacts the planet.

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, Function, Change, Responsibility, Sustainability

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Energy demonstration using a bouncing ball, light switches, candle (fire), and paper airplane. Students are asked to predict what connects these things and what the next unit is about.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Watch a video introducing what energy is.
- Sorting task: different energy sources (Energy Sort).
- WWWWH (Who, What, When, Where, How) linked to videos about energy, sources of energy, and energy transformation.
- Building electric circuits: students create their own circuits in pairs and experiment with breaking the circuit.
- Digital circuits activity on Seesaw.
- Whole class or modeled gist statement: How is electricity made?
- Watch video to 1:10 and brainstorm uses of fossil fuels.
- Solar Energy writing exemplar: plan, organize, and write about how solar energy works and why it is important.
- Visit the Tug of War poster and discuss/vote on the provocation "We need fossil fuels to power our lives."
- Watch videos on the pros and cons of using fossil fuels; students write pros and cons on post-it notes and display them.
- Watch video on how burning fossil fuels impacts the environment; final vote on Tug of War.
- Final vote in the form of a Gist Statement using BUT conjunction.
- Writing exemplar on fossil fuels: define, state pros and cons, and give an opinion.
- Seesaw tasks.
- Introduce STP using solar energy and coral reef exemplars.
- Library resources, Clickview video "Bang Goes the Theory: Human Power Station," "Energy is everywhere" by Patricia Walsh, Dr Binocs video, Solar Schools website, and picture story book about energy.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Monitoring, documenting, and measuring scientific language connected to energy sources.
- Displaying understanding of energy transfer within a circuit.
- Applied understanding of solar energy importance in writing.
- Developing understanding of energy sources and forming opinions.
- Writing self-assessment rocket rubric.
- Collaborating and analyzing circuits.
- Think Pair Share on energy sources.
- Seesaw tasks (including a specific HTWW task).
- Formative assessment throughout all tasks.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Student agency is supported through voice (describing/explaining energy sources), choice (circuits), and ownership (writing).
- Opportunities for self and peer assessment are embedded (writing self-assessment, collaborating on circuits, Think Pair Share).
- Transdisciplinary connections include links to science, writing, and digital technologies.
- Technology integration includes Seesaw for digital circuits and writing tasks, online videos, and interactive resources.
- Students expressed concern about the world not using more sustainable energy sources and wanted to know more about circuits and energy sources.
- The unit led to further investigation into the impact of fossil fuels and cleaner energy, connecting to the Sharing the Planet (STP) unit.

ci: To sustain life, people and environments depend on one another.
lo: Interdependence of people and environments; How people's wants and needs affect the land; Responsibility to use sustainable resources; Connection between fossil fuels and environmental impact
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: People need to consider the resources they use in order to look after the planet.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The resources needed to sustain life; Different resources are used to sustain life; How people look after the land is connected to their wants and needs; People's wants and needs define how they look after the land; Sustainable resources need to be shared.

KEY CONCEPTS: Responsibility, Connection, Causation, Sharing, Wants and needs

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: How is electricity made? Jot down main ideas as whole class or model gist: How is electricity made? Students listen to film music: Star Trek V and Star Wars and listen for rhythm patterns within Space words. Use Twinkl bilingual resources on the four seasons in the Northern Hemisphere to introduce key vocabulary and concepts.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Watch video to 1:10 about how electricity is made.
- Brainstorm all the things we use fossil fuels for.
- Visit the Tug of War poster and discuss/vote on the provocation: "We need fossil fuels to power our lives."
- Students compare seasonal patterns in the Northern and Southern Hemispheres, identifying similarities and differences in how the seasons change.
- Watch "Pros and cons of using fossil fuel" video.
- Reflection activity: Pros and cons ? post-it note write one for each, display in shared space.
- Maths activity: seasons and months.
- Listen and discuss: Star Wars movement activity (music and movement), Star Trek V listening and drawing response.
- Students learn about popular summer activities in both China and Australia, then create their own written piece explaining why they like summer.
- Watch "How does burning fossil fuels impact our environment?" and final vote on Tug of War.
- Final vote in the form of a Gist Statement TEMPLATE using BUT conjunction.
- Reflect on solar energy: What is solar energy? What are the pros and cons? How/why do we need to consider this instead of electricity?
- Introduce STP using solar energy and coral reef exemplars.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Ongoing: Peer assessment (class discussion, informational writing), observation (vocabulary use in individual and group tasks), formative assessment (in the moment feedback throughout lessons).
- Documenting: Weekly explicit instruction & practice (vocabulary), CFU - weekly grammar & what are fossil fuels?, BBS sentences, informative writing.
- Summative assessment: Informative writing, Seesaw task - Tug of War.
- Opportunities for self and peer assessment: Informational writing, discussion, sticky note opinions.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Library resources and guides are provided.
- Use of ClickView and YouTube videos for content delivery.
- Integration with Chinese (months, seasons, summer activities), Music (listening and movement), and Maths (seasons and months).
- Student agency is supported through lived experience with electricity, choice in Tug of War activity, and ownership in informational writing and opinions.
- Some students began asking about whether everyday devices could be charged by the sun rather than using electricity, indicating opportunities for student-initiated action and further inquiry into renewable energy.
- Transdisciplinary connections to previous and concurrent units (HTWW, ECLC, Prep, Year 1).
- Technology integration opportunities: Seesaw for assessment, digital resources for research and writing, music and video analysis, and possible exploration of solar-powered devices.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Journeys can lead to challenges and new understandings.
lo: Types of journeys (physical, learning, cultural); Challenges encountered on journeys; New understandings gained through journeys
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Not specified.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Not specified.

KEY CONCEPTS: Not specified.

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Photo Sorting & planning; Picture Storybooks: The Snail & the Whale, Little Chelone, I am Sangoel, The Gruffulo, Alfred's War (connects to ANZAC Day); PowerPoint & Template.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Photo sorting and planning using picture storybooks to explore journeys, opportunities, challenges, and change/growth.
- Reflection activities using templates (Reflection Template, Wave Template).
- Developing family interview questions for home learning about a significant journey, focusing on type, reason, timing, location, opportunities, challenges, and growth (using WWWWH syntax).
- Journey writing using a PowerPoint.
- Reflection on challenges, opportunities, and growth in a journey (learning, physical, or cultural) using Seesaw.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Monitoring: Peer assessment (class discussion, story CSPACE discussion, parent interview), observation (vocabulary use in individual and group tasks), formative assessment (in-the-moment feedback), documenting (wave templates, WWWWH, CSPACE, journey writing, modeling worked examples and student examples).
- Measuring: Summative Assessment (not further specified).

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Student agency is supported through student selection of journey, interview questions, and sharing personal journeys.
- Opportunities for self and peer assessment using a CSPACE rubric.
- Emphasis on communication skills (developing questions, synthesizing responses), literacy (summarising and inferencing), and transdisciplinary connections (personal, learning, and cultural journeys).
- Integration of digital tools such as Seesaw for reflection and assessment.
- Note: There was not enough writing time due to introducing something new.
- Student-initiated action: Students connected the ROAR framework to the unit and took more risks, e.g., speaking in assembly.

ci: The way we interact with others is influenced by who we are
lo: Personal identity and strengths; How self-regulation supports learning; Building resilience through knowledge of ourselves
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: The qualities we admire inform the choices we make. Everyone has different needs and they are met in different ways. We can build our ROAR through knowledge of our strengths. To learn effectively we need to be able to self-regulate. Using our strengths and self-regulation strategies enable us to bounce forward.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The qualities we admire inform the choices we make; Everyone has different needs and they are met in different ways; We can build our ROAR through knowledge of our strengths; To learn effectively we need to be able to self-regulate; Using our strengths and self-regulation strategies enable us to bounce forward.

KEY CONCEPTS: Causation, Perspective, Strengths, Self-regulation, Resilience

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Essential Agreement activity (students co-construct classroom agreements); Self Portrait Template; Wifi - Ready to learn activity.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- All About Me Selfie
- Personal Strengths activity (Frames of Strength PowerPoint and template)
- BOB it up booklet with podcast (Podcast link provided)
- Cyber Safety Incursion with SAFE poster
- Calm Palm Template
- Cyber Safety Lessons
- Mind Monsters activity
- UR STRONG lessons: Friendship fires, Growth mindset
- Letter to my Year 3 Teacher (reflection and transition activity)
- Students offer reflections on each other's games, discussing what worked well and possible improvements

ASSESSMENT: 
- Ongoing teacher observation and response to behaviours in class
- Discussion and reflection on frames of strength
- Personal goals setting and review
- Anecdotal notes shared through weekly briefing
- Letter to my teacher
- Ready to learn application
- Peer and self-assessment through game reflections

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Voice, choice, and ownership are embedded throughout all activities (e.g., students choose how to get ready to learn, create their own calm palm, and write a letter to their teacher)
- Opportunities for self and peer assessment are built in (e.g., letter to teacher, ready to learn application, game feedback)
- Students reflect on their strengths and self-regulation strategies, and how these impact their learning and social connections
- Transdisciplinary connections include social-emotional learning, digital citizenship (cyber safety), and transition to Year 3
- Technology integration opportunities: Seesaw for reflections and sharing, digital templates for self-portrait and calm palm, podcast listening, and possible digital creation of games or reflections

ci: Methods of communication have different features which impact their effectiveness
lo: The forms of verbal, non-verbal and visual communication; How the correct form enhances the effectiveness of a message; How people interpret messages and bring their own meaning to interactions
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: The correct form of communication can enhance the effectiveness of the message.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Forms of communication; Perspective?people can interpret messages and bring their own meaning to interactions; Persuasion?people can strategically use language and visual platforms to create compelling arguments.

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, Perspective, Persuasion, Communication

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Forms of communication - see, think, wonder. Have examples of songs and rhymes used in class music on the board to discuss during class. Constant referral to notation, metre markings, tempo and dynamics. Think, Pair, Share reasons that the following categories of people communicate: Friends, Family, Neighbours, Teachers, Newsreaders, Radio DJs, Reporters, Online Influencers, Interstate or Overseas friends or family, Interstate or Overseas businesses. Match the best method of communication with each.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Seesaw Forms of Communication task
- Class discussion and visual brainstorm
- Morse code, braille, Auslan (Library)
- Tally of the forms of communication that Year 3 uses
- Arranging dynamics and tempo to show increasing or decreasing volume and/or speed
- Listening and responding to change in metre to show 2, 3 and 4 beats to the bar and changes from a walking to swaying feel (Maths books)
- Tie in with UR Strong and Cyber Safety Project
- Begin to incorporate the use of dynamics and articulation (legato/staccato) in their compositions or when performing songs and pieces (Web programs)
- Responding to the lines of inquiry in a Unit Reflection (Seesaw - Talk - Presentation)
- Students are responsible for planning and rehearsing their own silent movie, choosing location and characters, and organising costumes and rehearsals
- Use of books from the library, LibGuides, and YouTube clips (e.g., Mr. Bean Official)

ASSESSMENT: 
- Monitoring students' responses in connection to forms of communication and purpose of communication within discussions
- Evaluate quality of written response to the appropriateness of different forms of communication (advantages and disadvantages)
- Students' initial responses documented by the teacher in the communal space and students' additional responses monitored
- Summative assessment Seesaw task: students articulate at least two different forms of communication, their purpose, and their intended audience
- Students used developed success criteria to complete a self and peer assessment of their silent movie and 2-minute talk

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Opportunities for self and peer assessment are embedded (silent movie, 2-minute talk)
- Informal collaborative activities are encouraged
- Student agency is supported through choice of elements in composition and performances, choice of communication form, and ownership of silent movie project
- Transdisciplinary connections: PE (communication through games and sport), Visual Arts (hieroglyphics & symbols), Music (signs, symbols, and music terms), Dance and Drama (non-verbal communication), Library (storytelling)
- Technology integration: Seesaw for reflection and portfolio, web programs for music composition, YouTube for examples, LibGuides for research
- Teacher reflection notes that the unit may need an overhaul for future years, suggesting a focus on platforms themselves (print vs digital media, sustainability) rather than bias and media literacy, which may be too sophisticated for Year 3

ci: Ethical decision making supports online choices.
lo: How choice of action and reaction affects others online; How informed choices can make a positive difference; Our right to feel safe online and the responsibility to make ethical decisions
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Choice of action and reaction affects others.

LINES OF INQUIRY: What is an ethical decision?; How do we achieve a balance between online and offline experiences?; How do we use digital media at home?; What platforms do different family members use and why?; Who is this app/website appropriate for?; Why do we need rules when online?

KEY CONCEPTS: Connection, Perspective, Responsibility, Rights, Relationships, Choices

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Discussions about online usage within the family; Cyber Safety Project Initial Lessons; Timon and Pumbaa Safety Smart Lesson.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Discussions about online usage within the family.
- Cyber Safety Project Initial and subsequent lessons.
- Timon and Pumbaa Safety Smart Lesson.
- Categorising findings from a homework task (e.g., communication, information, recreating).
- Creation and application of an Online Essential Agreement.
- Using and applying the essential agreement and cyber safety skills.
- Reflecting on choices.
- Students take iPads home and create a student-generated iPad essential agreement in response to learning.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Teachers monitor student responses to Cyber Safety experiences and evaluate how embedded the learning is.
- Student written and recorded responses show evidence of the depth of their understanding.
- Observation of appropriate choices and behaviour.
- Self-assessment: Students self-assessed how well they follow the essential agreement with traffic lights evaluation and examples provided.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Ownership: Discussions and reflections supported home to school connections and drew on student knowledge and thinking.
- Voice/Choice/Ownership: The iPad essential agreement was student created in response to learning.
- Opportunities for self and peer assessment.
- Resources include Library Guides and the Cyber Safety project.
- Students engaged strongly with Safety Smart online video and reminded each other to follow the essential agreement and that things posted online are there forever.
- Students monitored each other and alerted the teacher if a peer was not being safe online.
- Numerous examples of students living out the essential agreement or self-managing their behaviour to stay safe online came about after this unit. Students taught their siblings about being safe online, discussing elements of Cyber safety sessions.
- Transdisciplinary connections: English, Social Studies, Digital Technologies, Health.
- Integration opportunities: Digital citizenship, ethical decision making, online safety, student-created agreements, home-school technology connections.

ci: Materials behave and interact in certain ways, which poses challenges and solutions
lo: The three states of matter and their properties; How matter changes states depending on particles, temperature and energy; Responsible and ethical use of materials like glass and plastic
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Matter is all around is. There are three main states of matter ? solids, liquids and gases.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Matter is all around us; There are three main states of matter ? solids, liquids and gases; Matter can change states depending on the particles it is made up from; Materials can be convenient, but if they are misused or disposed of incorrectly, they can damage the environment; How a material and its usage changes over time and is adapted to suit different needs (such as glass and plastic); That products and materials work with their surroundings to be useful.

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, Change, Responsibility, Adaptation, Interdependence

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Watch Dr Binocs - Matter and other introductory videos (What is Matter ? General Science for Kids, States of Matter ? General Science for Kids, States of Matter and Change ? General Science for Kids). Use discussion questions to determine which statements are true or false. Observe and discuss the physical properties of a candy bar using the five senses.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Watch and discuss introductory videos about matter and its states.
- Use observation charts to explore physical properties of matter with a candy bar.
- Read about the three states of matter and find real-life examples, taking photos and explaining them.
- Watch videos and read about problems caused by solids turning into liquids (e.g., glaciers melting), brainstorm solutions, and design one idea.
- Conduct an Oooblek lesson.
- Melt ice cubes and measure variables for melting.
- Complete Seesaw tasks related to matter.
- Watch a video on how to make chocolate and create a flow chart to explain changing states of matter.
- Complete a reading comprehension on the history of chocolate.
- Research BTN Glass Usage.
- Participate in Inspire Lab activities.
- Complete a unit portfolio piece in the matter lesson plan.

ASSESSMENT: Ongoing assessment through Seesaw tasks, observations, group discussions, and regular revisiting of the central idea and lines of inquiry. Final assessment was not completed in 2024, but ongoing assessment through discussions and small group work showed a general understanding of all lines of inquiry.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Student agency was supported through voice (developing questions and conducting research using BTN), choice (discussing soft plastics usage at home and alternatives), and ownership (collecting soft plastics in the classroom, using reusable containers for Nude Food Day).
- Opportunities for self and peer assessment were not present in this unit for this year.
- Resources included Library Guides, BTN ? Glass, BTN ? Plastic, Seesaw 'Matter planner', and BTN: Science Week 2022.
- Technology integration included Seesaw for portfolio tasks, digital photo documentation, and online research (BTN, YouTube).
- Students began collecting soft plastics in the classroom and encouraged recycling.
- Transdisciplinary connections to Science, English, and Design and Technologies.

ci: People can respond to natural disasters with resilience
lo: The causes and types of natural disasters (tectonic events, weather events); How natural disasters impact the environment and people; How humans respond with resilience and connection
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Natural disasters impact the environment and people.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The causes of natural disasters; Humans respond to natural disasters and strive to move forward; Natural disasters impact the environment and people; People come together in the face of adversity.

KEY CONCEPTS: Causation, Resilience, Impact, Connection

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Brainstorm in pairs ? Prior Knowledge assessment. Start reading Michael Morpurgo's "Running Wild" ? A story about a boy who survived the Boxing Day Tsunami. (Note: In 2024, students could read at home.)

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Brainstorming and prior knowledge assessment about natural disasters.
- Reading and discussing "Running Wild" by Michael Morpurgo.
- Grouping and categorizing types of natural disasters (tectonic, weather, illness events).
- Completing worksheets explaining and grouping natural disasters.
- Watching and discussing videos on tectonic plates, earthquakes, volcanoes, tsunamis, cyclones, hurricanes, typhoons, tornadoes, floods, and wildfires (various YouTube and Behind the News resources).
- Drawing tectonic plates on a world map and answering related questions.
- Investigating how Japan prepares for earthquakes and the impact of natural disasters on culture.
- Learning and reciting a verse from "My Country" by Dorothea MacKellar.
- Analysing features of a newspaper and writing a newspaper article about a well-known natural disaster (e.g., Cyclone Tracy).
- Creating a Natural Disaster Survival kit and participating in earthquake preparation activities.
- Conducting a science experiment related to natural disasters.
- Maths activity: Measurement of disasters.
- Seesaw Volcano Investigation.
- Case study: Japan ? how Japan gets ready for an earthquake.
- World map activity showing frequency of natural disasters.
- Design tasks: Design Comprehension 1, 2, 3, and a Design Brief.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Ongoing monitoring, documenting, and measuring of learning through observation, group discussion, and regular revisiting of the central idea and lines of inquiry.
- Self-assessment and peer feedback on creations (newspaper or design).
- Summative assessment combining STP and WWAIPAT, supporting connections across transdisciplinary themes and learning areas (Mathematics and English).
- Students research, plan, and write a newspaper article about a natural disaster.
- Reflection stems for self-assessment: What have I done well? What am I particularly proud of? What could I improve?
- Peer feedback on newspaper articles or designs.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration with English (report writing), Science (natural phenomena), Geography (mapping, case studies), and Mathematics (measurement).
- Use of Seesaw for portfolio pieces and video resources for independent and group research.
- Opportunities for student agency: students choose the natural disaster to research, the sources to use, and the format for presenting their findings (Keynote or poster).
- Students grouped by shared interests for collaborative inquiry.
- Action opportunities: students consciously included features to minimize the impact of natural disasters when designing buildings in the next unit.
- Resources include Japanology from YouTube, Library Guides, and a range of curated video content.
- Technology integration: Seesaw for investigations and portfolio tasks, online videos for research, and digital presentations.
- The unit supports transdisciplinary connections, especially with the following unit on city design and disaster resilience.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Many factors shape the development of cities.
lo: The essential features of a city; The function of a city; The development of cities through time
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Cities adapt and change over time due to their demographic, density and social development.

LINES OF INQUIRY: How a city works; Structures needed for buildings in disaster prone areas; A community needs infrastructure to function effectively; How infrastructure brings people together; Musicians and composers are inspired by their surroundings to create works.

KEY CONCEPTS: Function, Structure, Community

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Students watch a ClickView video about transportation (in Chinese), connect to previous units and their knowledge about natural disasters, and create a city with no planning using post-it notes and paper. This unplanned city is then used to discuss the needs for a city to function.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Watch Mega Cities videos and Chinese videos about transport in the city.
- Incursion from Mr Rory Hyde (Architect and Lecturer at Uni of Melbourne) about planning in Shepparton and floods; students use knowledge from this speaker to add features to their city and adjust their thinking about city layout.
- Chinese: Travel Project and learning facilities names in Chinese.
- 3D building design to withstand natural disasters.
- Venn diagram comparing Mexico City and Paris; revisit New York and Tokyo.
- Record sounds from various parts of the city and make QR codes to bring the city alive with sounds.
- Research cities around the world, find their location on a map, and with a partner draft and write a comic/graphic page to include in a class book.
- Creation of a class city including natural disaster knowledge; label facilities in Chinese on the class city project.
- Building and maths reflection (portfolio task).
- Design comprehension tasks and design brief.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Ongoing assessment of students' ability to select and place purposeful buildings in a city, considering function, density, and green space.
- Assessment of understanding and response to geographical obstacles in city and building design.
- Creation of a brochure (in Chinese) demonstrating transport choices, sequencing, and time connectives.
- Portfolio tasks including building and maths reflection, design comprehension, and design brief.
- At the Student Led Conference (SLC), students explain the process of building in a geographically challenged environment and justify the placement of three city features.
- Opportunities for self and peer assessment, including reflection on city effectiveness and peer feedback on designs.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong transdisciplinary connections, especially with Chinese (transport, facilities, brochure creation), Visual Arts (3D building design, clay work), Music (city soundscapes, Mussorgsky's "Pictures at an Exhibition"), and Mathematics (density, green space, proportions).
- Integration of technology through QR codes, sound recording, and digital comic/graphic creation.
- Student agency is supported through choice of building type, design, placement, and research topics; students create their own travel agency and house out of clay.
- Action opportunities include students sharing their work at home and with families, and applying learning from excursions/incursions (e.g., Shepparton flood planning).
- Resources include YouTube (Megacities of the World), library guides, and guest speakers.
- The unit is highly engaging for students, with practical application of research and design, and strong links to real-world issues such as natural disasters and urban planning.

ci: The creative process involves understanding and applying artistic elements to communicate ideas and emotions across disciplines
lo: How colour, movement and music communicate character and mood; The creative process of making a multi-disciplinary presentation; The effort and commitment required to produce a live performance

After the chat, students produce a question bank plus a short perspective-and-evidence summary that compares the chatbot responses with unit sources linked explicitly to How We Express Ourselves.","url":""},{"t":"Makey Makey","d":"Using the Design Cycle, students prototype interactive character masks or props that trigger sound effects or music when touched, integrating Makey Makey with conductive materials to physically express character traits and emotions in a live performance context.","url":""}]
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Not specified.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Not specified.

KEY CONCEPTS: Not specified.

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Chinese: Students will explore the expression of colours in different art forms. 'See-think-wonder' thinking routine: Beijing Opera.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Chinese: Students to compare the meaning of colours in different cultures.
- Students will explore the meaning of different colours in expressing the characters' traits on Beijing Opera masks.
- Students will create their own character mask using colours to express different personalities.
- Students study poses of the Chinese opera that match the corresponding character. The students recreate these poses and add their own interpretation. Students then justify their decisions in relation to their new character and their knowledge of the Beijing Opera tradition.
- In class, students will write a "script" for the character of their choice to support the design of the performance for the Beijing Opera.
- Students work in a partnership to create a vignette. Clown, warrior, villain and female character. No dialogue, body acting. Seesaw template will have our story, film, reflection.
- Yuxuan opera: Begin to write at the beginning of Year 4.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Monitoring the student's ability to compose, choreograph and refine collaboratively with their peers.
- Developing confidence and polish in their performance.
- Ability to act on feedback to improve their performance.
- Review student self-reflections.
- Students created Success Criteria to assist them when reflecting on where to next.
- Students reflected via Seesaw tasks and were given regular opportunities to build and improve on given feedback during rehearsal time (Music/D&D).
- Students wrote a reflection about areas of success in the artwork. Shared viewings facilitated discussions about student style and personalisation. Opportunities to further improve artworks (at the student's initiation) were facilitated.
- Throughout the preparation process, students were given opportunities to reflect and discuss ways of improving their work. This included refining their stories, making sure colour choice aligned with their character personality (masks), students used exaggerated actions in their performance and incorporated listening and watching when performing their musical ensemble.
- Students had the opportunity to reflect on their piece during the Seesaw task. Class viewed all performances and listened to all music and gave feedback on how well the actions and music represented their story.
- A selection of students performed their pieces at assembly.
- Performance included mask, story, music, movement was recorded and uploaded as a Seesaw Portfolio task.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Powerpoint and shared portfolio piece used as resources.
- Integration across Chinese, Dance and Drama, Music, English, and Digital Technologies.
- Use of Seesaw for reflection, story, film, and feedback.
- Students select traditional characters, create a story, choose instruments, create mime actions, design their own mask, and select the colour splash and place it in a unique way.
- Self-curated artworks and self-assessment.
- Opportunities for students to perform at assembly and share with the wider community.
- Transdisciplinary connections with art, music, drama, and Chinese cultural studies.
- Technology integration includes using Seesaw for digital portfolios, reflection, and sharing performances.

CENTRAL IDEA: Not specified.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Not specified.

KEY CONCEPTS: Function, Causation, Responsibility, Citizenship

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Not-for profit organisations prior knowledge - What do I think I know? What would I like to find out? Scenario about different family groups to work out wants and needs.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Investigating Not for Profit organisations: Students work in partners to research two not-for-profit organisations, recording information on a data chart (organisation name, function/purpose, who they help, local/national/global).

- Visits to Junior School classes to promote planned actions.
- Group work to facilitate and promote actions.

- 1:1 conferencing to evaluate research skills.
- Evaluation of written and oral responses.
- Work samples, posters, and discussions.
- Seesaw tasks: Not For Profit organisation research task, research links, and action.

- Students watch bridge testing and discuss findings.

ci: First Nations people have a deep connection to Country, language and culture
lo: The diversity of language and culture across First Nations communities in Australia; The significance of connection to Country as custodial responsibility; Listening to and learning from Indigenous perspectives
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Students create interactive 3D artefacts using Merge Cubes and the iPad camera, overlaying their own First Nations-inspired symbols and stories. They record oral storytelling in their own voices, then use the Merge Explorer app to bring their artefacts to life, allowing peers to 'hold' and explore each other's stories in augmented reality.","url":""},{"t":"Canva","d":"Students design digital posters or infographics that compare and contrast different First Nations countries, languages, and cultural practices across Australia, using Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to integrate their own artwork or photos of their paintings.","url":""},{"t":"iMovie","d":"Students produce short documentaries or creative retellings of Dreamtime stories, combining footage from their Melbourne Museum excursion, voiceovers, and digital artwork to reflect on the importance of Country and cultural diversity.","url":""},{"t":"Book Creator","d":"Students collaboratively author a digital book that weaves together personal reflections, research, and original artwork about First Nations perspectives on Country, language, and sustainability, embedding audio explanations and music soundscapes they have created.","url":""},{"t":"Padlet","d":"Students build a collaborative digital map on Padlet, pinning locations of different First Nations countries, adding student-created videos, soundscapes, and artwork to each region to showcase the diversity and connection to Country.","url":""},{"t":"Lego Spike Prime","d":"Using the Design Cycle, students prototype and build a 'sustainable gathering tool' inspired by First Nations ingenuity, then document their process and test their designs for environmental impact and efficiency, reflecting on how traditional knowledge can inform modern STEM solutions.","url":""}]
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: First Nations people connect to land (Country), language and culture.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The impact of changing the physical environment; The inventive nature of First Nations people in their design of tools and systems that support the gathering of food; The importance of the connection to Country for First Nations people; There are similarities and differences in the language and culture of First Nations people across Australia; Traditions are an important part of indigenous culture.

KEY CONCEPTS: Connection, Perspective, Diversity, Tradition, Identity, Responsibility

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Students explore the concept of "Country" through a definition and discussion, watch a video of desert people and take notes on similarities and differences between these people and themselves (see page 25). They use Seesaw to record their thinking.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Brainstorming the meaning of diversity and mapping different First Nations countries, comparing them to countries in Europe.
- Excursion to the Melbourne Museum (First Nations exhibition), using worksheets from the indigenous folder.
- Reading "Australia from Dawn to Dusk" by Brentos and "Welcome to Country" by Auntie Joy Murphy and Lisa Kennedy.
- In groups, creating a soundscape representing a page in the book, focusing on time of day, animals, scenery, location in Australia, and respect for First Nations Australians.
- Comparing two indigenous countries using Venn diagrams and Seesaw tasks.
- Compiling questions to ask a student from the studio school, with plans to spend more time with them in 2026.
- Viewing and discussing performances, reflecting on what worked well and how to improve.
- Creating an acrylic on canvas painting featuring First Nations symbols and their own symbols, telling a personal story.
- Creating digital and hand-drawn representations using iPads, Sketchbook app, and iMovie.
- Visit from the Indigenous Wesley Program.
- Using resources from Ochre, Indigenous Knowledge Resources for Australian School Curricula Project, and picture books such as "Wilam William Barton Birdsong at Dusk".

ASSESSMENT: 
- Ongoing monitoring of students' ability to research and represent information in their own words, visual observations of applied techniques, and teacher observation during Student Led Conferences.
- Painting plan uploaded to Seesaw with detailed artwork storyline.
- Evaluation of students' ability to retell the Bunjil creation story through writing and artworks.
- Assessment of students' ability to articulate the importance of trees in First Nations communities and to synthesize versions of the Bunjil creation story.
- Writing a four-paragraph piece titled "colonisation or invasion".
- Group work communication and soundscape reflection.
- Informal peer discussions and feedback on artworks and performances.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration of digital technology: students use Seesaw for reflection and documentation, Sketchbook app and iMovie for creative expression, and iPads for research and artwork creation.
- Transdisciplinary connections with Science (environmental balance and sustainability), Music (soundscapes), Visual Arts (symbolic painting), and Social Studies (First Nations history and perspectives).
- Action opportunities include sharing knowledge with parents and the wider school community, and student-initiated visits to exhibitions and sharing stories at home.
- Opportunities for student agency through choice of presentation format, creation of personal symbols, and responsibility for gathering and presenting information.
- Plans to deepen engagement with the studio school and extend time for student interaction in future iterations of the unit.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploration and discovery have led to changes that shape the world we live in
lo: The age of exploration and the reasons explorers set out on their journeys; How Australia changed through European arrival; Different perspectives on the consequences of exploration

They design, build, and program a Lego Spike Prime robot to follow a mapped route representing an explorer's journey, iterating on their design to overcome obstacles and simulate real-world exploration challenges.","url":""}]
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Australia (landscape, demographics, identity) has changed significantly in the last 200 years

LINES OF INQUIRY: What Australia was like pre and post European arrival; The varied perspectives on European arrival; The positive and negative consequences of exploration; New discoveries can come about through exploration

KEY CONCEPTS: Change, Form, Perspective, Impact, Exploration

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Unpacking the central idea and making predictions about what the unit will be about; Looking at a series of different maps and trying to order them from oldest to newest to explore the age of exploration (see map activity and links on page 45)

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Viewing an Inquisitive video about exploration
- Using laminated cards to research various explorers and trace their movements on maps, then completing a Seesaw task
- Researching explorers in Chinese (Seesaw activity)
- Presenting researched information to peers and organizing it in a data chart display to identify patterns and make connections
- Reflecting on the impact of European arrival on Australia from a range of perspectives
- Indigenous perspective of Cook's arrival covered in the next unit

ASSESSMENT: 
- Students complete independent research tasks to identify who/what/did/when/how information about explorers, then use this information to write their own extended paragraph including all relevant information in full sentences
- Peer and self-assessment through structured feedback moments at the end of collaboration sessions
- Peers provide feedback about note taking and respond to suggestions
- Self and peer swap research into explorers to identify key elements (WHO, WHAT, WHEN, WHY?)

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Student agency is supported through voice (creating a text that shows what they have learnt), choice (choosing the explorer to research from a limited list), and ownership (choosing design ideas, headings, images, and text to include)
- Opportunities for structured feedback and peer review are built in
- Resources include Inquisitive, Seesaw research, and Library Guides
- Transdisciplinary connections with English, History, Geography, Civics and Citizenship, and Social Studies
- Technology integration includes Seesaw for research and presentation, and use of digital maps and videos

ci: Leaders can help a group to function effectively
lo: Roles and responsibilities of effective leadership; Purpose of leadership; The strengths and qualities of a leader
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: They have an opportunity to make change through: self-initiated action, supporting the leadership of others, responding to the actions of another. Different points of view add to the richness of possibilities and support us in making decisions that are considerate the whole community ? with perspectives beyond their own. They are leaders, with or without a badge. Leaders role model the learner profile.

LINES OF INQUIRY: What makes a good leader; What do good leaders do; What are the jobs of the different leadership roles we can go for.

KEY CONCEPTS: Responsibility, Perspective, Leadership

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Break up the central idea, what does it mean to be a leader. What does "work effectively" mean in this context. Brainstorm examples of people who are leaders. Select one leader and reflect on what makes this person a good leader (e.g., Mrs Debono).

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Partner and group brainstorms to define qualities of effective leaders and create lists of attributes.
- Sorting activity: students select an attribute and explain its importance and demonstration.
- Creation of an Essential Agreement based on the ROAR acronym, with partner work to explain and connect to respect, resilience, etc.
- Role-play and visual tasks: students show what leadership attributes look like in different contexts (e.g., listening in class/assembly).
- Reading and reporting on "Little People, Big Dreams" books, including guided and independent report writing.
- Staff leaders present on leadership portfolios; students list required attributes and create personal portfolio applications.
- Students create and present iMovie pitches for leadership roles.
- Peer and teacher feedback on leadership applications and pitches.
- Presentations of completed videos.
- Cross Campus Leadership Day and use of resources like "Rise Up ? Jackie Scott".

ASSESSMENT: 
- Formative: Observation checklists during group activities, peer and self-assessments, and reflective journals.
- Summative: Create a presentation or project showcasing an effective leader and their qualities. Role-play scenarios where students demonstrate leadership skills.
- Peer feedback on leadership applications (2 stars and a wish).
- Teacher feedback on Seesaw tasks (e.g., Little People Big Ideas Seesaw Task).

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Student agency is supported through collaborative brainstorms, choice of leadership roles and iMovie pitch, and ownership of behaviour and leadership actions.
- Peer and teacher feedback is embedded throughout.
- Technology integration includes Seesaw for tasks and reflections, iMovie for pitches, and digital presentations.
- Transdisciplinary connections to PE, Music, Dance and Drama, Library, English, Civics and Citizenship, Social Studies, and Health.
- Opportunities for student-initiated action, such as fundraising tasks.
- Resources include Anthony Albanese provocation, Little People Big Ideas Books, Library Guides, Teacher Mentors, and cross-campus events.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Goods and services are exchanged within a marketplace.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The production of goods; The exchange of currency for goods and services; Ethics in the marketplace.

KEY CONCEPTS: Function, Connection, Economics, Supply and demand, Distribution.

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Students define, connect, and extend business and financial terminology by writing meanings under words they know, drawing linking lines between connected words, and creating a mind map of the central idea. They consider the lines of inquiry and use paired fluency to read a text and answer questions. There is also a focus on sustainability games such as The Fish Game, Power Our Community, Stop a Disaster, and Journey 2050 to introduce concepts of sustainability and systems thinking.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Mind mapping and vocabulary activities to connect business concepts.
- Playing sustainability simulation games (The Fish Game, Power Our Community, Stop a Disaster, Journey 2050).
- Supply & Demand activities.
- Types of Resources ? Sam's Soccer Ball Factory activity.
- Creating production chains for a chosen product, showing all stages from raw materials to retail.
- Watching BTN clips about cost of living and inflation, completing cause and effect worksheets.
- Collaborative research and role play on ethics in the marketplace.
- Launch Suburb project: students design a suburb (name, logo, floor plan), vote on names, set up jobs, write job applications, and manage roles.
- Setting up student businesses, writing business proposals, trading goods and services, and using receipts.
- City Trading Launch: students participate in trading sessions across three suburbs.
- Ongoing roles and business management within homeroom time.
- Use of spreadsheets and OneNote for budgeting and business tracking.
- Maths Probability Carnival and Financial Maths Investigation workshops.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Monitoring spreadsheet use (scheduled and surprise checks).
- Weekly meetings to debrief progress in roles.
- Reconciling receipts with spreadsheets.
- Reviewing business proposals.
- Financial Maths Investigation & Workshops.
- Maths Probability Carnival.
- Portfolio Tasks ? HWOO Evaluation / Reflection, Production Chain, Job Application Letter.
- Seesaw Task - Unit Evaluation.
- Peer and self-assessment through classroom discussions, town and city meetings, and analysis of business profit and loss.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration with Maths (financial maths, probability), English (job applications, business plans), and digital technologies (spreadsheets, OneNote).
- Opportunities for student agency: students choose business ideas, jobs, and roles; design and run their own businesses; manage budgets; and enact roles in a simulated economy.
- Technology integration includes spreadsheets for budgeting and business tracking, OneNote for documentation, and Seesaw for reflection and assessment.
- Emphasis on ethics, collaboration, and self-management skills.
- Students are encouraged to bring in props and create businesses at home, connecting classroom learning to real-life experiences.
- Transdisciplinary connections to Civics and Citizenship, Economics and Business, Social Studies, and Digital Technologies.
- Opportunities for action include creating and running businesses, reflecting on ethical decisions, and managing personal spending and saving.

ci: Inventions and initiatives are a response to the changing needs of society
lo: How inventions have changed over time to meet the demands of society; The UN Sustainable Development Goals and the global problems they address; Using the design thinking process to create innovations that respond to real-world needs
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Inventions and initiatives are a response to the changing needs of society.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The needs of society change over time; Inventions are a copy, transformation or combination of existing ideas; People can problem solve to address needs in society; The design process and scientific method.

KEY CONCEPTS: Function, Change, Innovation, Transformation, Society, Design.

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Students look at different inventions of the past and answer questions about them (name, year/decade, purpose, evidence for their hypothesis). They then break down the central idea by highlighting significant words, brainstorming, and connecting images to these words.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- "What's That Invention?" activity (analysing past inventions)
- Breaking down the central idea with images and brainstorming
- "Changed Over Time": Students choose a modern invention, research its history and changes, and describe significant changes over time with images
- Science lab sessions: Exploring elements of inventions (Light Boxes and Steam experiments)
- SDGs: Ordering, grouping, and unpacking activities
- Watching "Everything is a Remix" and analysing ideas
- SDGs further investigation: Students choose 3 SDGs to research using infographics and tables
- "Shark Tank": Analysis of ideas
- Following the Design Process in groups (Empathise, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test), using PowerPoint and Canva AI for digital designs
- Guest speaker: Anna Hancock (expert in Climate Change) gives a presentation
- FTF (Future Thinkers' Forum): Students present their inventions and receive guest feedback

ASSESSMENT: 
- Monitoring student articulation of SDG priorities and understanding of global issues
- Checklist completion
- Invention timelines
- Parent feedback from the FTF
- Portfolio tasks, presentations at the Future Thinkers' Forum, self and peer assessment during the forum

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong focus on student agency: Students select SDGs, form groups based on interests, choose problems and design solutions, and present in various formats (digital/physical)
- Integration of digital tools: Canva AI, PowerPoint, Seesaw for documentation and reflection
- Transdisciplinary connections to science (lab sessions), SDGs, and design thinking
- Opportunities for action: Students share research websites, design inventions for real-world problems, and present to the community
- Noted need for more time with technology (3D printing, VR) in future iterations
- Resources include UN SDGs, YouTube (Shark Tank, BrainPOP), library research tools, picture story books, and guest speakers

ci: Communities interact with the environment in different ways
lo: How community values shape the management of the natural environment; The need for conservation and environmental stewardship; Ways in which First Nations land management practices can drive positive environmental change

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Different people's connection to country influences their perspectives and responsibilities towards the environment.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Different people's connection to country (European and indigenous); Animals, themselves and indigenous connections to the land; That they have a responsibility to conserve the environment and make change; How they can conserve different parts of the environment; What the features of different environments are; What people are doing to drive change and how they can be a part of that.

KEY CONCEPTS: Connection, Perspective, Responsibility, Conservation, Environment, Stewardship

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Unpacking the Central Idea; Gallery Walk; Vocabulary - Environments. Students unpack the central idea, participate in a gallery walk to observe and discuss images or information about environments, and engage in vocabulary activities related to environments.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Exploring different environments using worksheets and fact sheets.
- Investigating conservation and stewardship, including an Indigenous focus with lessons on Indigenous land management.
- Writing emails to trees as part of the Melbourne Urban Forest initiative.
- Chum Creek & ECOSS Visit (excursion to learn about stewardship and conservation in practice).
- Mapping world biomes and examining native gardens and species at Wesley College.
- Watching clips about First Peoples' action in caring for the land (e.g., traditional burning).

ASSESSMENT: 
- Monitoring understanding of indigenous connections to land and country, unpacking vocabulary, and observing native gardens.
- Documenting through mapping tasks of world biomes, camp booklets (Seesaw ? Portfolio task), and writing emails to trees.
- Measuring individual understanding of key terms (management, conservation, stewardship) and learning in Seesaw tasks about changing the natural environment, reasons for conservation, and human impact.
- Feedback given via Seesaw; ongoing formative assessment on class activities.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Action opportunities include student-initiated improvements to gardens around the Year 5 'Rose Garden' and collaboration with Environment Prefects for native planting.
- Transdisciplinary connections with Science, Geography, History, Civics and Citizenship, Social Studies, and Design and Technologies.
- Integration of digital tools such as Seesaw for portfolio tasks and reflections, and use of the Melbourne Urban Forest website for research and email activities.
- Opportunities for self and peer assessment through poster creation, reflection on responsibilities to the land, and sharing perspectives of First Nations Peoples.
- Suggestion for future years: expand activities around how First Peoples cared for and continue to care for the land.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Colonisation has had a lasting impact on different countries and their people
lo: The reasons, methods and impact of colonisation; How colonisation transformed society and culture; The responsibility of colonisers and the perspectives of those colonised
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Students create interactive 3D Merge Cube experiences that allow users to explore different perspectives of colonisation?such as the viewpoint of Indigenous peoples, colonisers, and contemporary Australians. Students then app smash by embedding their Merge Cube AR videos and reflections into a multimedia Book Creator eBook, presenting a layered, immersive narrative of colonisation's impact.","url":""},{"t":"Padlet","d":"Launch a collaborative digital timeline where students post research, images, and primary sources about colonisation in Australia and other countries. Each post can include student voice recordings, artwork, or short video reflections, allowing for a rich, multi-modal exploration of the central idea.","url":""},{"t":"Canva","d":"Students design persuasive infographics or digital posters that highlight the lasting impacts of colonisation on different countries and their people. Canva's 'Remove Background' feature can be used to create striking visuals that juxtapose pre- and post-colonisation imagery.","url":""},{"t":"Clickview","d":"Curate a playlist of documentary clips and historical re-enactments about colonisation. Students use ClickView's interactive video features to annotate, ask questions, and respond to key moments, deepening their understanding of causation and change.","url":""},{"t":"Minecraft Education","d":"Students explore the museum of key innovations from history, then research and build their own 'inventions' that either resulted from or responded to colonisation. This activity encourages students to consider how technological and cultural changes were shaped by, and in turn shaped, colonisation.","url":"https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/challenges/the-hall-of-inventions"},{"t":"3D Printers","d":"Using the Design Cycle, students design and 3D print artefacts that represent both Indigenous cultures and colonising societies. They prototype, test, and refine objects such as tools, symbols, or monuments, then present their creations alongside a reflection on how these objects tell the story of colonisation's impact.","url":""}]
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: The reasons, methods and impact of colonisation; Transformation of society & culture due to colonisation; The impact of the choices of colonisers and those colonised.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The reasons, methods and impact of colonisation; Transformation of society & culture due to colonisation; The impact of the choices of colonisers and those colonised.

KEY CONCEPTS: Causation, Change, Responsibility

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: After reading "The Rabbits," students use a sunglasses template to draw and write from the perspective of different groups involved in colonisation. They answer questions about motivations, experiences, changes, and responses. Students also break down the central idea by highlighting significant words, brainstorming, and connecting images to the central idea.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Reading and perspective activity using "The Rabbits" and a sunglasses template.
- Breaking down the central idea with brainstorming and image connections.
- Research and summarising: Watching an Australian History video and completing a template.
- Creating a class mini SAT example on Britain and individual research templates.
- Using ClickView resources on the impact of colonisation on Indigenous Australians.
- Conducting research on Britain using a provided link.
- Identifying modern examples of colonisation/invasion (e.g., World War II, Israel-Palestine).
- Making connections between the class novel "Convict Boy" and the unit of inquiry, as well as Tom Appleby's memories.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Monitoring understanding of the Central Idea and key concepts.
- Documenting learning with a mini SAT on Britain.
- Measuring understanding through a Seesaw Task as SAT.
- Oral presentations of research with peer assessment and feedback.
- Reflection on final tasks, identifying areas of success and development.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Library resources accessed via WiSE tile; library sessions focus on research skills and accessing suitable websites.
- Students have choice in which country to research and present about, supporting agency.
- Students create their own PowerPoint and oral presentation, with peer feedback.
- Student-initiated action includes discussions about current political unrest and questioning colonisation vs invasion (e.g., Russian invasion of Ukraine, Gaza-Israel conflict, USA bombing of Iran).
- Opportunities for technology integration include using Seesaw for assessment, PowerPoint for presentations, and ClickView for video resources. Library sessions support digital research skills.

ci: Values, beliefs and actions contribute to a diverse society
lo: How our values and beliefs shape our identity and interactions; Our responsibility to manage emotions and be safe online and in person; The importance of resilience, respect and positive relationships in a diverse community
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Students design a 3D interactive 'Values & Beliefs Cube' using Canva to create each face, representing different values, beliefs, and actions from their own lives and diverse cultures. These are then uploaded to Merge EDU, allowing students to explore each other's cubes in augmented reality, sparking discussion about diversity and empathy in a hands-on, immersive way.","url":""},{"t":"Padlet","d":"Create a collaborative digital 'Beliefs Wall' where students post multimedia reflections (videos, images, audio, text) about their own values and beliefs, and respond to classmates' posts to explore the diversity within their classroom community.","url":""},{"t":"Book Creator","d":"Students author digital autobiographies focusing on their values, beliefs, and actions, integrating photos, voice recordings, and drawings to express their unique identities and how these contribute to a diverse society.","url":""},{"t":"Wise Discussion Chatbots","d":"Students interact with a Self-Reflection Wise Discussion Chatbot in the role of 'Samira, a digital citizenship mentor'. They respond to prompts like 'Describe a time your values influenced your actions online' or 'How do you show respect in digital spaces?' After the chat, students create a personal action plan outlining strategies for managing emotions and building respectful online communities.","url":""},{"t":"Adobe Express","d":"Students design visually striking digital posters or infographics that showcase the importance of respect, inclusion, and understanding in a diverse society, using Adobe Express's templates and 'Remove Background' feature for creative self-portraits.","url":""},{"t":"Lego Spike Prime","d":"Using the Design Cycle, students work in teams to prototype a 'Respect Robot' with Lego Spike Prime. The robot must perform a task that symbolically represents inclusion or empathy (e.g., delivering messages of kindness or bridging two groups), requiring students to ideate, build, test, and refine their solution while reflecting on how technology can promote positive actions in a diverse community.","url":""}]
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Wellbeing is influenced by the connections we have with ourselves and others.

LINES OF INQUIRY: 
- The connection between beliefs, values and actions;
- How we show respect for ourselves and others;
- Strategies for building positive relationships.

KEY CONCEPTS: Responsibility, Form, Connection, Identity, Relationships, Safety

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Essential Agreement, t-shirt about me, "Find someone who..." Bingo, GYM-Meet the animals in your brain.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- 16 lessons of CyberSafety
- 11 UR Strong lessons
- 5 Grow Your Mind lessons
- Seesaw Tasks: CyberSafety - Create your own golden rule, GYM - Sharing problems, Seesaw Evaluation
- Activities include: Essential Agreement, t-shirt about me, Bingo, GYM-Meet the animals in your brain, group activities, class discussions, roleplay options, goal setting, and reflection tasks.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Monitoring: Learner Profile attributes when self-assessing collaborative tasks.
- Documenting: ATL and Learner Profile certificates given at assembly.
- Goal Setting: portfolio task, morning routines, desk tags for reminders, portfolio feedback review.
- Measuring: Portfolio Task (reflection), Seesaw Tasks (Sharing problems, CyberSafety - Golden Rule, Evaluation).

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Action opportunities: Students used strategies from the programs to resolve their own issues, discussed online difficulties with trusted adults, and shared knowledge of online scams.
- Transdisciplinary connections: Wellbeing at Wesley, Cyber Safety, URStrong, Grow Your Mind, Health and PE, Chinese, French, Choral, English, Mathematics, Civics and Citizenship, Social Studies, Design and Technologies.
- Integration notes: Weekly sessions and group activities using URSTRONG, CyberSafety, Grow Your Mind; Seesaw used for assessment and reflection; class assemblies and collaborative tasks for student voice and ownership; technology used for online safety awareness and digital portfolio tasks.

ci: Understanding global issues and using effective communication empowers us to raise awareness and inspire action.
lo: Global issues are caused by interconnected factors that require collective solutions (Causation) ??? Presentations in the media can reflect the perspective of the author rather than the truth (Perspective) ??? We must make responsible choices when responding to and creating media (Responsibility) ??? Messages can be expressed through a range of mediums and elements can be adjusted to enhance effectiveness (Communication) ??? Design choices contribute to the effectiveness of a message (Influence)

These multimedia books can include embedded videos, audio reflections, and clickable links, serving as both a portfolio and a tool for advocacy.","url":""},{"t":"Minecraft Education","d":"Students explore how technology can be used to protect and celebrate cultural heritage sites, drawing parallels to their own campaigns for global issues. They reflect on the importance of collaboration and digital storytelling in raising awareness and inspiring action. https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/peter-is-here-ai-for-cultural-heritage","url":""},{"t":"Lego Spike Prime","d":"Using the Design Cycle, students prototype a kinetic installation or interactive model that visually represents their global issue and proposed solutions.
